flashcards.back: An algorithmic paradigm for solving combinatorial optimization problems that systematically enumerates solution space while eliminating infeasible or suboptimal solutions through intelligent pruning strategies.
The process of dividing the problem into smaller subproblems (creating child nodes) to explore the solution space systematically.
Computing the best possible solution value (lower bound for minimization, upper bound for maximization) that can be achieved from a given node.
Eliminating subproblems whose bounds indicate they cannot yield a better solution than the current best, thus discarding them without further exploration.
A node that has been generated but not yet expanded (its children have not been generated). Also called a pending node.
The node currently being expanded - a live node whose children are being generated or about to be generated.
A node whose bound is better than the current best solution, indicating potential for finding an improved optimal solution through further exploration.
A node whose bound is worse than the current best solution, making it impossible to find a better solution - such nodes are pruned.

flashcards.front: Difference between FIFO and LIFO in B&B
flashcards.back: FIFO (Queue-based) explores level by level (BFS), suitable for minimization problems. LIFO (Stack-based) goes deep first (DFS), may find good solutions faster but less systematic.

flashcards.front: What is Lower Bound in minimization problems?
flashcards.back: The minimum possible cost that can be achieved from any solution in the subspace represented by a node - used to prune if it exceeds current best.

flashcards.front: What is Upper Bound in B&B?
flashcards.back: The best (lowest cost for minimization, highest profit for maximization) solution found so far; also serves as a threshold for pruning.

flashcards.front: Why is B&B better than brute force for TSP?
flashcards.back: B&Bprunes entire branches that cannot yield shorter paths than current best, reducing worst-case from O(n!) to much smaller search space.

flashcards.front: What is the 0/1 Knapsack problem solved by B&B?
flashcards.back: Given items with weights and values, select subset to maximize total value within weight limit. Unlike fractional knapsack, B&B must explore all combinations as greedy fails.

flashcards.front: How does B&B differ from Backtracking?
flashcards.back: Backtracking explores all possibilities until solution found; B&B uses bounds to prune suboptimal branches, avoiding unnecessary exploration.

flashcards.front: What is the Job Assignment Problem in B&B context?
flashcards.back: Assign N jobs to N workers at minimum cost - B&Bprunes assignments where partial cost already exceeds best known solution.
